title: Tantrāloka Volume 10 — Āhnikas 28-29: Occasional Rites, Death, and the Secret Observance
subtitle: Dyczkowski translation (2023). The examination of death, states of initiates at death, the Kaula secret observance, the tantric consort, and initiations by piercing.

summary.one_line: Abhinavagupta's Āhnikas 28-29, translated by Mark Dyczkowski (2023). Āhnika 28 covers occasional rites, the examination of death (mṛteḥ parīkṣā) with detailed analysis of the states of initiates at...
summary.scope: Covers Āhnika 28 (death examination — first appearance of life, uninitiated vs. initiated at death, yogis who do not experience death, death as entry into another body, embodied and disembodied liberation, power of creative imagination); Āhnika 29 (the secret observance — Kula liturgy, definitions of Kula, wine as nectar, the daily Kaula ritual, the Kula maṇḍala, the twelve Kālīs, the sixty-four Yoginīs, the tantric consort, the processes within consciousness during union, the six initiations by piercing: by mantra, by sound, by the point, by empowered piercing, by the serpent with five hoods, by the supreme piercing).

sources.label: Tantrāloka of Abhinavagupta, Volume 10 (Āhnikas 28-29), translated by Mark S.G. Dyczkowski (2023). Full PDF, 52 MB.

body.text: Āhnika 28's examination of death (mṛteḥ parīkṣā) is the most detailed account of death in the Tantraloka. It covers: the first appearance of life and the process of death and decay; the states of uninitiated vs. initiated souls at death; yogis who do not experience death (they transcend it while alive); death as yogic entry into another body (paradehapraveśa); embodied liberation (jīvanmukti) vs. disembodied liberation (videhamukti) at the moment of death. The key teaching is that the state of consciousness at death determines the post-mortem state — not karma alone. (Āhnika 28, vv. 217-367, pp. 97-165)

Those who die in sacred places (āyatana) without initiation share the world of the deity (sālokya) but do not attain liberation. The initiated who die in sacred places attain proximity to the deity (sāmīpya). The initiated who possess knowledge attain the state of conjunction (sāyujya) — union with Śiva. Various kinds of initiates have different fates at death depending on the completeness of their knowledge and the intensity of their grace. Some attain liberation immediately at death, others pass through intermediate states, and still others are reborn to complete their practice. (Āhnika 28, vv. 237cd-291, pp. 105-126)

The yogī who has mastered the subtle body can enter another body at death (paradehapraveśa). This is not a supernatural feat but a natural consequence of the yogī's identification with consciousness rather than with his physical body. The technique involves withdrawing the vital breaths and the mind into the central channel, then projecting consciousness into a suitable receptacle. The ability to do this is a sign that the yogī has realized the illusory nature of the physical body and has attained the divine body (divya-deha). However, Abhinavagupta warns that this should not be the goal — the goal is liberation, not extended existence in any body. (Āhnika 28, vv. 294-324, pp. 127-139)

Jīvanmukti (embodied liberation) is liberation while still alive — the state of the one who knows 'I am Śiva' while still inhabiting a body. Videhamukti (disembodied liberation) is liberation at the moment of death, when the last trace of karmic impurity dissolves with the body. Abhinavagupta argues that both are true liberation — the difference is not in the quality of realization but in the duration of the residual body. The jīvanmukta lives out the remainder of his karmic momentum without being affected by it, like a potter's wheel that continues spinning after the potter has stopped. At death, the jīvanmukta attains videhamukti — the body dissolves into consciousness without any residue. (Āhnika 28, vv. 308-351ab, pp. 132-154)

The power of creative meditation (bhāvanā) determines the state at death. The reality of what one sees in a dream, the power of the creative imagination, and the perceivers who are dreaming — all of these are levels of bhāvanā. At death, the mind's last creative act determines the next state. The yogī who has trained in bhāvanā can consciously direct his consciousness at death, just as one can train to direct dreams while asleep. Those who have not trained are carried by karmic momentum. The highest practice is to maintain the bhāvanā 'I am Śiva' continuously, so that at death this recognition is spontaneous. (Āhnika 28, vv. 351cd-367, pp. 154-165)

Āhnika 29 on the Secret Observance (rahasyacaryā) is the most esoteric chapter of the Tantraloka. It describes the Kaula rites that are only to be taught to those of the highest capacity. The definition of Kula is given: Kula is the totality of all existent things seen as the body of the goddess; Akula is Śiva beyond all manifestation; the union of Kula and Akula is the supreme Trika. The Kaula sacrifice is not mere ritual but the recognition of all things as the play of consciousness. Wine is the nectar of the left (the lunar current), the liquid offering is the life-force, and the lamps are the three eyes of Śiva. (Āhnika 29, vv. 1-17, pp. 200-221)

The Kula maṇḍala is the assembly of deities, teachers, Siddhas, and Yoginīs arranged in a specific configuration. At the outer level are the eight Mothers (Mātṛkās) and eight Bhairavas; then the sixty-four Yoginīs; then the twelve Kālīs; at the centre are Kuleśvarī and Kuleśvara. The four Yuganāthas (Matsyendranātha and others) preside over the six lineages. The sixty-four lamps for the Yoginīs represent the sixty-four energies of consciousness. The worship of the eight Mothers and Bhairavas is the foundation of the Kaula liturgy. (Āhnika 29, vv. 24-55, pp. 227-251)

The tantric consort (dūtī) is the embodiment of Śakti in the Kaula rite. The qualities of the consort, the kinds of consort, and the protocols of union are described in detail. The processes within consciousness that take place in the course of union (melaka) are analyzed: the climax of the couple in union in the stable abode of emission, quiescence and emergence, the perpetual expanding abode of Śakti, the essential being of emission (visargasattā) and the consumption of the ejaculate. This is not mere physical union but a yogic practice in which the vital breaths of the partners merge, creating a single circuit of consciousness — the 'stable abode of emission' where thought ceases and only the pulse of consciousness remains. (Āhnika 29, vv. 96-166ab, pp. 284-345)

The six initiations by piercing (vedha-dīkṣā) are the most direct form of initiation, where the guru 'pierces' the disciple's subtle body to awaken consciousness. According to the Kulagahvara tradition: (1) Piercing by Mantra — the guru's mantra enters the disciple's heart; (2) Piercing by Sound — a specific sound vibration opens the central channel; (3) Piercing with the Point — the guru's gaze or a pointed object concentrates the disciple's awareness; (4) Empowered Piercing — the guru transmits śaktipāta directly; (5) Piercing by the Serpent with Five Hoods — the five prāṇas are awakened as Kuṇḍalinī; (6) The Supreme Piercing — direct recognition without any external support. According to the Vīrāvalikula, there are nine piercings including piercing of the worlds, piercing with form, piercing of the body, piercing of place, and piercing of the channels. (Āhnika 29, vv. 236-291, pp. 377-411)

The five levels of sacrifice (yāga) are: (1) the external sacrifice with ritual substances; (2) the sacrifice based on Śakti (the tantric consort); (3) the sacrifice based on the couple; (4) the sacrifice based on the body — where the body itself is the maṇḍala and the vital breaths are the offerings; (5) the sacrifice based on the vital breath — where the breath is the oblation; (6) the sacrifice based on the mind — where thought is the offering. Each higher sacrifice subsumes and transcends the lower. The true Kaula practitioner performs the internal sacrifice of the body, breath, and mind, recognizing that the external rites are supports for those who cannot yet internalize. (Āhnika 29, vv. 166cd-186ab, pp. 345-353)

gaps: Full analysis of the specific verses on each category of initiate at death
Complete extraction of the Kaula liturgy steps; the sixty-four Yoginīs names and attributes

issues.title: Death states comparative mapping
issues.description: Volume 10's death examination (Āhnika 28) is a major primary source for the death systems convergence RO. The detailed categories of initiates at death should be cross-referenced with Tibetan Bardo, Egyptian afterlife, and Steiner.

issues.title: Vedha-dīkṣā comparative mapping to daimon path
issues.description: The initiations by piercing (vedha-dīkṣā) have direct parallels to the daimon path's 'resistance' phase where the practitioner is pierced by the daimon's presence. This should be extracted and mapped.

versions.summary: Initial RO created from Tantrāloka Volume 10 (Dyczkowski, 2023). 10 passages covering the examination of death, initiate states at death, yogic entry into another body, embodied vs. disembodied liberation, bhāvanā at death, Kaula secret observance, Kula maṇḍala with Yoginīs, the tantric consort and union, initiations by piercing, and the five levels of sacrifice.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state from newly acquired Tantrāloka Volume 10 PDF (52 MB, ~33,618 lines text). This adds the death examination and Kaula secret observance to the corpus.
